nodes.label: Water the plants
nodes.desc: Direct manual watering of the garden. While it keeps plants alive, it consumes potable water resources and requires time.

nodes.label:
Use Synthetic
Fertilizer
nodes.desc: Boosts flower growth and market value quickly, but degrades long-term soil microbiome and leaks into groundwater.

nodes.label:
Manual
Hand-Weeding
nodes.desc: Removing weeds by hand avoids toxic chemicals, but is extremely labor-intensive and slows down expansion.

nodes.label:
Plant Monoculture
Cash-Crops
nodes.desc: Planting only the highest-selling flower species maximizes revenue but destroys local biodiversity and resilience.

nodes.label:
Water
Conservation
nodes.desc: Fresh water is a finite resource. We must minimize consumption to protect the local watershed.

nodes.label:
Soil
Living Health
nodes.desc: Soil is a living ecosystem. We must avoid synthetic chemicals that kill beneficial microbes and fungi.

nodes.label:
Chemical-Free
Ecosystem
nodes.desc: Pollinators and birds depend on a toxin-free environment. We prioritize organic methods over pesticides.

nodes.label:
Species
Diversity
nodes.desc: A resilient garden requires a variety of plants to support different insect species and prevent total crop failure.

nodes.label:
Maximum
Revenue
nodes.desc: The primary goal is to generate high sales to fund further garden expansion and agent upgrades.

nodes.label:
Lean
Operations
nodes.desc: Minimize external costs by using recycled materials and avoiding expensive store-bought supplies.

nodes.label:
Labor
Efficiency
nodes.desc: Time is money. The agent should minimize the number of actions required to produce a sellable product.
